This area had historically been used for quarrying, leaving the subsoil with large, loosely packed areas of dirt. Now today nearly every new building is constructed using this method, but at the time it made use of a relatively new technique called slip forming. It had been developed in the US in the early 20th century to build agricultural structures like silos, but by the 1960s it was being used in high rise buildings. It works by constructing a mold, which concrete is continuously poured into. As the lower layers sets, hydraulic jacks push the mold up, creating a continuously growing building. Now one of the biggest benefits of slip form construction is the speed. The core of the Tormon Panas was under construction 24/7, growing at a rate of around 30 centimeters every day.
